segments.text: Morning
Session now, uh,
and hearing submissions today on the Fast Track approvals
Amendment Bill, uh, and first up, today we have Forest
and Ver welcome Richard to the Select Committee.
Good morning this morning. Uh, you are with us
for 20 minutes this morning, so, uh,
take your submission as read.
Of course, uh, members will have questions,
but we'll hand over to you for your opening remarks.
Uh, good morning. Thank you, Madam Chair.
Uh, Tana Carto, Carto, everybody.
Uh, and thank you for this opportunity to speak.
Uh, my name is Richard Kappy.
I'm Chief advisor at Forest and Bird,
and fingers crossed joining me online, uh, is, uh,
Peter Anson, uh, barrister, uh, who has been advising Forres
and Bird on a number of the fast track applications
that are currently in train.
Since the Fast Track Approvals Act came into force, Forrest
and Bird has been engaged constructively in a number
of significant fast track projects.
We have experience from the front line
and several decisions.
Fast track panels themselves have explicitly acknowledged
the value that organizations like Forest
and Bird are bringing to the process.
These examples demonstrate that their independent expertise
improves outcomes, reduces environmental risk,
and actually increases public trust.
Against this background, it is extremely concerning
that the Amendment Bill would effectively shut out such
advice from future fast Track
decision if passed.
This bill removes the ability for panels to provide
or invite in input from experts, NGOs, or community groups.
Unless councils decline to comment.
It blocks invited experts
and NGOs from appealing clear legal errors.
It allows applicants to influence who sits on panels
deciding their applications,
and it concentrates decision making
in the hands of ministers.
It also collectively reduces legal oversight
and public transparency.
These are very material decisions that will lead
to poor decision making
and greater long-term risk to communities,
ecosystems, and the crown.
Before I talk a little bit more in detail about our
concerns, I'd like to make some comments about the process
that this legislation has been following.
Uh, as you know, this bill began, uh, with a bit
of a narrow kind of punt around grocery competition.
I note the gross the term grocery is mentioned once in
substantive clauses, specifically Clause 59.
The bill itself focuses on the fast track process
as a whole, uh, with the specific policy
for supermarket competition outlined separately,
I would suggest that if the government really wishes
to improve grocery competition,
they should focus legislation to achieve that purpose.
Instead, this bill made without public input
and without a regulatory impact assessment significantly
alters New Zealander's Democratic rights
to participate in decision making.
And bear in mind that these are, by their very nature,
under the original legislation nationally
and regionally significant,
these are landscaping community altering decisions
that will last generations.
There was no analysis
of these changes in the related treasury briefings,
yet they have major implications for public participation.
Ian Harley writes, environmental safeguards
and administrative Fairness.
The amounts of time provided to Parliament
and the New Zealand public to consider
and comment on legislation of this important
and magnitude is totally unacceptable.
The number of submissions on the original legislation
clearly show the level of interest
and show the strength of feeling around fast track.
Uh, IMY Sins was one of 27,000 people
who marched down Queen Street in protest against
the original legislation.
The level of interest in this bill is very significant.
The amount of time that people have got to comment on it
and participate in this process is negligible.
Returning to the draft legislation itself,
a critical concern for forest
and bird is the concentration
of powers in the hands of ministers.
The bill would allow ministers
to issue government policy statement defining what counts
as quote unquote regional
or national benefit panels would be required
to consider these statements.
Something that in effect,
let's minister predetermine the benefits of a project,
while panels will then only be responsible
for assessing the environmental costs.
This goes against the very logic that this committee
and Parliament relied on when shifting decision making from
ministers to independent panels last year.
These ministerial statements require no public input,
require consultation only
with ministers whose portfolios match the subject matter,
excluding environmental ministers significantly.
They also give ministers disproportionate control
over panel outcomes.
To all intents
and purposes, they vest ministers with the ability
to pick winners and greenlight projects.
The risks flagged during the original legislation remain
removing transparency and openness, undermining checks
and balances on executive power,
and furthering open further opening up the prospect
of lobbying and conflicts of interest.
Forest and bird strongly recommends removing these clauses.
While this proposed change is perhaps the most concerning.
There are other changes in this propo in this bill
that are perhaps just as bad.
The bill removes the requirements to consult council's,
agencies, iwi and hapu before lodging applications.
This is inconsistent with Titi
and risks reducing Maori involvement in decisions affecting
reducing com comment timeframes to 20 day from 20 days
to 15 days, makes it harder for agencies
with statutory environmental responsibilities
to provide robust evidence-based advice.
Again, thinking about the nature of these projects,
it feels like we are trying to shoot ourselves in the foot
by reducing the amount of time to gather
and collect evidence to make good decisions.
The bill denies NGOs
and community groups, the appeal ability
to appeal on legal errors,
even when a panel invites their input.
Errors of law will instead be pushed into judicial review,
which is slower, more expensive and counterproductive.
So if the intent behind this bill is to speed things up, uh,
I think you need to think again.
Requiring potential panel members names to be given
to applicants invites panel shopping
and actually fundamentally undermines the
independence of decision makers.
This is most egregious, allowing ministers
to change schedule two by order of council
is constitutionally inappropriate
and bypasses parliamentary scrutiny on project scope,
allowing applicants to substantially modify an application
with no opportunity for further expert input risks panels
approving projects never considered at the referral stage.
So taken together these changes,
silence independent environmental expertise,
reduce transparency, diminish democratic participation,
increase legal and environmental risk.
Weak can checks on ministerial
and applicant's applicant influence,
undermine public confidence in environmental decision
making, and ultimately, they will lead
to worse decision making.
You don't improve the ability to get things right
for massive, complex projects like a new tunnel
through the middle of Wellington
or a new hydro dam by putting in place arbitrary
and unrealistic timeframes
and limiting your own ability to seek information.
Do we really think it only requires three times as long
to consider an application for a new Mount Victoria Tunnel,
as it does to consider an application
for a new house on Mount Victoria?
This is not a minor technical amendment.
It is a significant weakening of public
and environmental safeguards
and will lead to worse decision making.
Accordingly, forest
and Bird asked the committee to remove provisions,
enabling government policy statements on benefits,
remove restrictions on panels, inviting expert comment,
preserve full rights of appeal on points of law,
retain meaningful consultation requirements with councils,
iwi and affected parties, remove provisions,
allowing applicants to influence panel appointments.
Remove the ability
to amend Schedule two via order in council,
allow panels flexibility in decision timeframes,
and provide further comment when applicants are
materially modified.
Finally, I'd conclude by noting Forrest
and Bird has proven that constructive expert public input
improves decisions and reduces environmental harm.
The member Bill would reduce our, remove that voice.
We urge the committee to protect transparency,
democratic rights,
and the health of our environment
by rejecting the most damaging provisions of this bill.
Failing to do so will make this law unsupportable
and remove any semblance of legitimacy.
This, in turn, significantly increases the justification
and likelihood that decisions made under this law will be
revisited and won't be recognized by a future government.
Nahed Nui, uh,
for Bird would welcome the opportunity to answer your questions.
Thank you.

expertise in a specific area.
And there's actually a requirement proposed
to be added in the bill to require panels, to have someone
with expertise in the, essentially the,
the subject matter area of the, um, application.
So if you're looking for a specific, um,
area expertise in a specific area,
and you are also, um, needing someone who's available
to do quite a lot of work fairly intensely in a particular
timeframe and, um, doesn't have any confidence of interest,
then that may require, you know, going to quite a number
of people before you actually find, uh,
somebody who's, who's able to do it.
And that all takes time, um,
which is difficult if you've now got a very limited
timeframe for setting up the panel.
Thank you. Thank you, uh, Ryan, with the next question.
Oh, thanks Chair. Uh, Sarah, in your, um, submission,
you talked about how this proposed process is gonna
streamline some of the, um, I guess processes
and doing, doing them concurrently
and the view that it will speed up
and make it more efficient by up to six weeks.
Your argument was that it's potentially gonna, uh,
make it more challenging
and slow the process down and inefficient.
So I was just wondering where that rationale is coming from.
Thank you. Um, so the rationale for the, the submission
that, um,
counterintuitively the amendments could actually slow things
down, uh, is that there are a number of,
while there are a number of streamlining provisions added,
there are actually also some that, um,
will inherently add additional steps.
So for example, there's a proposal for the ability
to contest, uh, the suitability of panel members,
and there are, um, working days assigned for that process.
Or in fact, there, there are no working days assigned.
There's no time limit assigned for one of those parts
of the process, which is
for applicants to provide their comments.
So that's adding an additional step that is not currently,
um, dealt with in that way.
Currently, it's dealt with, um, through the convener calling
a, um, a conference of the parties to discuss that in a,
in a single, um, meeting.
So other examples where things could end up taking longer is
that if, uh, that the removal
of consultation from the beginning of the process
and replacement of that with notification.
Uh, generally in resource management circles, the earlier
that issues can be flushed out
and can be considered by an applicant,
the better an application tends to be in its substance.
So the replacement of consultation with notification early
is something that could counterintuitively end up with more,
um, time required later in the process to actually try
and tease out what those issues are
and for the applicant to then respond to them.
Uh, another one is the example that we've given
where if the appeal rights are removed from people
and uh, they take a judicial review instead,
then those would be two separate high court processes rather
than being joined as a single high court process.
And then that those two high court processes are being run
on completely separate timeframes
and completely separate proceedings.
So those are three.
Um, just, and earlier in your comments, you mentioned
that one of the new things
that's being proposed has no working days tagged to it,
so it's a bit, I guess, ambiguous.
But if we put working days on that, would
that not then clarify or time that process
It would for that particular steps.
So the law societies made specific recommendations in the
table for a number of improvements that could be made if,
uh, the provisions are gonna remain.
Uh, but the point being that that entire process
of contesting the appointment of panel members, um,
is an additional step in the process
that was not previously provided for.
Um, and there are other examples where, um,
extra time is going to be, uh, taken to resolve some
of these issues potentially, uh, where this,
the system currently, uh, does provide flexibility for those
to be, um, worked out before the application is lodged
or through, uh, the tling of individual application.
Thank you. Q, See, can I, I like to ask
to expand on section four
where you talk about the unusually broad powers
that this bill would give to ministers,
and in particular whether, um, their ability to
issue a government policy statement, um,
almost about a particular project rather than a really broad
policy statement, um, could open our
resource management systems up to accusations of corruption.
Uh, well, I think our, our concern here is essentially
that there is very little, uh, it's virtually nothing
provided in the bill, uh, regarding what,
what a government policy statement, uh, ca can do, uh,
beyond simply the statement, uh, that it, that it would, uh,
contain provisions regarding, uh, what qualifies as, uh, uh,
regional or na significant regional and national benefits.
Uh, so it's essentially an open-ended discretion
and we don't think it would be appropriate
for those statements to be targeted at, um,
particular projects
because obviously, uh, there's supposed
to be an independent process here
and, uh, it, it would be inappropriate for the minister
to be essentially saying
to the panel who's making a decision on whether there's
significant national or regional benefits, there's,
that there's significant national, regional benefits, uh,
for a particular project that would undermine the
independence of the decision making.
So, so what we think is important, uh, is to, is
for, to include some, some safeguards to ensure
that the project, um, is that the power isn't, um, able
to be used to make directions in relation
to a particular pro, to a particular project, um, rather
that it's supposed to be a more general power, um,
in addition to our recommendation re re uh, regarding, uh,
requiring consultation.
Thank you. Thank you.
Just time for one last question from Grant Pellum.
Yeah, very quickly, um, just you,
you talk about the timeframes is obviously a bit of concern
and particularly when referring to larger projects,
60 days is, might not be, um, long enough, I suppose, is
what, what would you consider a more realistic timeframe
for this, for the decision making process once you get to
that point for, from particularly for more complex projects?
Well, I think, I think the point that we'd make is
that it is quite dependent on the nature
of the particular project.
So, you know, some projects, 60 working days might be fine,
uh, other, other day, uh, other projects, uh,
really we think it's important
that the convener has the ability to set a, um, a,
a project, a a timeframe that's based on the,
on the, on the particular project.
And we have seen projects where the, the timeframe
that the applicant has in fact said, we think is, is,
is reasonable, is, is more than, uh, 60 days
because they recognize the complexity of it.
So it, it, it does depend, you know,
you're seeing some projects, um, where you know, it, it,
it might be longer than that might be, say,
a hundred working days.
But I think our position would be that rather than having a
specific, you know, arbitrary cutoff, there needs
to be some flexibility for the convener to set
what is necessary
for the particular project taking into account, for example,
the volume of information, the complexity, the, the number
of commenters and so on.
Thank you. Look, thank you very much.
We have met time today. We appreciate your time very much.
Your submission very thorough. So you have a good day.
Thank you. Thank you.
